title: How A Beauty Company Built An AI Agent — With Rachel Williamson and Josh Siebert
summary: Ulta Beauty deployed ServiceNow's AI-powered HR platform across its 65,000-person workforce, with leaders discussing automation decisions, human-in-the-loop design, personalization, and expansion to onboarding and employee lifecycle management. The episode offers insights into how a major retailer balances AI capabilities with human involvement in HR operations.
writeup: A sponsored interview at the ServiceNow Knowledge conference with Ulta Beauty's VP of People Strategy Rachel Williamson and VP of AI, Data, and Enterprise Platforms Josh Seabert, tracing the company's live April 8th deployment of ServiceNow as its HR service delivery platform for 65,000 associates. The core problem was fragmented, non-personalized HR content on a broken intranet that forced store floor workers to queue at HR desks during lunch breaks to answer basic policy questions. The solution centers on AI-powered search and a Now Assist chatbot that personalize responses by identity and location, replacing a static handbook that could never handle jurisdiction-level variation in employment law.
key_points: Ulta Beauty's 65,000 associates across stores, distribution centers, and corporate had no effective way to find HR policies on the intranet, with search so broken that employees were described as embarking on 'Lewis and Clark level expeditions' and queuing outside HR offices during breaks to get answers to basic questions like how much PTO they had.

The explicit design goal was to 'multiply Rachel's team' and scale HR support capacity without adding headcount, treating the AI deployment as a force multiplier rather than a replacement of any particular role.

A static employee handbook is insufficient at Ulta's scale because California employment law alone varies by city and county, so the ServiceNow platform identifies each associate's location and role to return the specific policy that applies to that individual rather than a one-size-fits-all answer.

Ulta deployed IT service management on ServiceNow first (under the internal name Shimmer), and the HR portal launched second, meaning associates now have a single surface for both HR policy questions and open IT cases such as cash register outages or field service issues.

The user interface is deliberately Ulta-branded (orange and pink) and surfaces both a Now Assist chatbot and an AI-powered search field side by side, with the search field cited as the biggest immediate crowd-pleaser over the prior intranet experience.

The platform is designed for three distinct personas: corporate workers, store floor associates, and distribution center workers, reflecting the constraint that most of the workforce are not desk workers and need to get answers on the floor, not from a desktop browser.
